note: GM-only lore. Editable from the console Wiki tab; grows with the campaign. Nothing here reaches player payloads.

nodes.title: Visseren
nodes.body:
The new world. Not empty — other towns, cities, and powers wait out in the dark hexes — but the settlers arrived knowing nothing and owning nothing. The name is what the crossing charts called the coastline; nobody remembers who wrote it on them.

Seed page — grow me as the map reveals itself.

nodes.title: The Old Country
nodes.body: Where the fifty came from, and what they don't talk about. Jory vouched for half the passage list at the harbor. Ellory's father was a harbor clerk ruined by one false ledger. Calder wore another army's colors once. Keep it vague at the table until a player digs — backstory is better excavated than announced.

nodes.title: The Crossing
nodes.body:
The voyage that made the fifty a people. One storm, remembered differently by everyone who survived it: Calder went into the water three times and came out with two; Lyra's brother was the third. Old Sef (proposed folk) saw something beneath the ship — and it saw him back. The passage manifest survives in Ellory's locked drawer: fifty names, one of which nobody answers to.

The storm is the campaign's shared wound. Touch it rarely.

nodes.title: The Settlement
nodes.body:
Fifty souls, five buildings, one palisade. The scale is intimate: every death is a name, one good foreman changes everything. The town is the players' character as much as their PCs are — its ledger is the campaign's memory.

Seed page — record founding decisions, layout, and the things the players name.

nodes.title: The Folk Web (who owes whom)
nodes.body:
The load-bearing secrets, in one place (full truth lives on each folk card's GM notes):

— Garrick owes Jory his passage; he has seen the bunkhouse joinery and says nothing.
— Jory's gift is people, not carpentry (§8C, the designed story — protect it).
— Calder is a deserter under a dead man's name; he carries the knife of the man he couldn't save.
— That man was Lyra's brother. She feeds his dogs. Neither she nor Calder has ever spoken of it.
— Ellory has noticed the manifest's extra name. She wants a reason before a judgment — but she will want one of the two.

Every thread crosses at least one other. Pull any of them and the web moves.

nodes.title: The Dark Hexes
nodes.body:
The map begins black; the players pick a direction and walk. Out there: other powers, older claims, and the rare-and-enormous kind of magic (§2 — a sword inside a log, a wolf that speaks). The scanned map and its locations legend live in docs/map/ — reveal hex by hex, never by accident.

Seed page — add rumors here before they're confirmed, one line each.

nodes.title: The Wheel of the Year
nodes.body: A year passes with each new story arc; within it the seasons turn with the building rolls. The wheel is the campaign's metronome — springs are for hope, winters collect debts. When the folk b-plot system lands, its scenes will ride this wheel (spring scene, then summer, autumn, winter).

nodes.title: The Bard's Tome (meta — deepest secret)
nodes.body:
The PCs are figures written into being by the Bard exactly as created. The players' tome IS his book; they write in the book that wrote them. Rewards were penned on their arrival page and never revised; the audience beyond the fourth wall (Suno listeners) applauds performances the characters cannot hear.

The god whose mind contains this world is properly named the Dreamer. The Bard did not arrive by accident: the Dreamer knew that Vessarin, their current muse and well of power, was dying, and invited the Bard into themself. They told him he needed only to wake Vessarin from her dream. The statement concealed the decisive truth: Vessarin was never the true source of creativity. Vesserin'ka is the Dreamer's true muse, but she is not the Dreamer.

The Bard accepted the stipulation that only the death or wakening of the dreamer could release him. Vessarin died, but her death changed nothing because the bargain's true referent was the god enclosing this place. Trapped and aware that he was deceived, the Bard has made a pact with the Abyss. He is obsessed with finding Vesserin'ka, believing she may provide a path to the Dreamer he is determined to face.

This is the campaign's floor. Nothing on an ordinary player surface should explain it — only hairline cracks: a first page in a hand nobody claims, a portrait that was always already there. Oore's cartography dispatch deliberately addresses the Dreamer without explaining the name. Preserve the distinctions: Visseren is the world, Vessarin the dead reservoir, Vesserin'ka the true muse, and the Dreamer the god whose mind contains them. Full design: docs/bard-systems.md.
